g.data-narration: Welcome to Program Status Register instructions in ARM microcontrollers, essential for embedded systems programming.
The Program Status Register is a 32-bit processor register that holds condition flags, processor mode, and control bits.
The PSR contains four condition flags in bits 28-31: N (Negative), Z (Zero), C (Carry), and V (Overflow).
Each flag has specific meaning: N indicates negative result, Z shows zero result, C tracks unsigned carry, V detects signed overflow.
CPSR holds current processor state in all modes. SPSR saves CPSR during exceptions and is only accessible in privileged modes.
g.text: Available in ALL modes | Holds current processor state
Privileged modes only | Saves CPSR on exception

g.data-narration: ARM provides 16 condition codes for conditional execution. These include EQ (equal), NE (not equal), HI (unsigned higher), GT (signed greater than).
MRS reads PSR to a register, MSR writes to PSR. MSR uses field specifiers: _c for control, _f for flags only.
g.text: PSR Transfer Instructions: MRS and MSR
Move from Status Register - Read CPSR/SPSR into Rd
Move to Status Register - Write Rd to CPSR/SPSR

g.data-narration: CMP performs subtraction without storing result, only updating flags. The S suffix on arithmetic instructions enables flag updates.
g.text: Compares R0 and R1 by subtracting R1 from R0
Updates flags but DOES NOT store result
Updates ALL condition flags based on result

g.data-narration: TST performs logical AND and updates N/Z flags. TEQ performs XOR and updates N/Z flags. Both clear C and V flags.
g.text: Logical AND: R0 AND R1 → flags only | Updates N, Z | Clears C, V
Logical XOR: R0 XOR R1 → flags only | Updates N, Z | Clears C, V

g.data-narration: B and BL instructions support conditional execution with all condition codes, enabling efficient branching without explicit comparisons.
g.text: BEQ label | BNE label | BGT label | BLT label | BHI label | BLS label
Branch instructions can have any condition code suffix for conditional execution

g.data-narration: This example implements unsigned greater-than: CMP R0,R1 compares values, BHI branches if unsigned higher when C=1 and Z=0.
g.text: CMP R0, R1 ; Compare R0 and R1, update flags
BHI greater ; Branch if unsigned higher (C=1, Z=0)

g.data-narration: This example detects signed overflow using the V flag. Overflow occurs when adding same-sign numbers produces opposite-sign result.
g.text: MOVS R2, R0 ; Copy R0 to R2
ADDS R2, R2, R1 ; Add R1 to R2, update flags
BVS overflow ; Branch if V=1 (overflow set)

g.data-narration: This example shows MRS/MSR for mode change: save CPSR, modify mode bits, write back with MSR_c to change to Supervisor mode.
g.text: MRS R0, CPSR ; Save CPSR to R0
ORR R0, R0, #0x13; Set Supervisor mode bits
MSR CPSR_c, R0 ; Write to control bits only

g.data-narration: Avoid common errors: forgetting S suffix, using signed conditions for unsigned values, accessing SPSR in User mode, wrong MSR specifiers.
In summary: PSR has N,Z,C,V flags for condition codes. Use MRS/MSR for PSR access, CMP/TST for flag updates, Bcc for conditional branches.
g.text: PSR = 4 flags (N,Z,C,V) + mode bits | MRS/MSR for PSR access | CMP/TST update flags | Conditional branches enable efficient code
Master PSR instructions for embedded systems success!
